imageCaption: A lighthouse beam that cycles through impossible colors — not quite light, not quite sound made visible — illuminating a family standing on a rocky Portuguese coast, their silhouettes glowing with the same frequency as the beam itself.
text:
The lighthouse was humming.

Not the beam—the whole structure, from the painted white tower down through the rock it was built on, was producing a sound that Aaliyah felt in her teeth more than heard in her ears. The frequency was almost identical to the one the Córdoba plaza had been singing, except slightly higher, slightly more urgent, like the lighthouse was trying to get their attention by slowly raising its voice.

"This is either a really good sign or we're about to have a catastrophic glitch encounter," Aaliyah said, which was basically her entire verbal toolkit at this point: observe danger, narrate danger, proceed toward danger anyway because her family had decided that was how they did things now.

"The dog agrees with you," Elijah reported. He was gripping Sully's collar with the focused intensity of someone managing a small furry seismic monitor. "Three short barks, but they're like... happy barks? Excited barks? Sully, are you excited about the lighthouse or are you warning us?"

Sully responded by tugging toward the lighthouse with the kind of enthusiasm that could've been interpreted as either profound wisdom or an urgent desire to investigate whether lighthouses stored treats in their foundations. With Sully, it was genuinely impossible to tell the difference.

The Portuguese coast stretched around them in all directions, dramatic and rocky and somehow exactly the kind of place you'd expect to find a glitch that had decided to live somewhere beautiful instead of in a parking garage or a highway overpass. The sky was that particular shade of evening that happened in the hours before sunset—not quite blue, not quite gold, existing in the transitional space where day and night negotiated their boundaries. The lighthouse was painted white and red in horizontal stripes, which meant it had been built long enough ago to follow the old maritime color codes, which meant it had been standing in this exact location making its intentional frequency for probably more than a hundred years.

Aaliyah's phone buzzed. ARIA-7. Of course ARIA-7.

*Fascinating. You've traveled directly to one of the original temporal anchor points. This lighthouse was constructed in 1887 by a Portuguese engineer who had no rational reason to build it on this precise location—the navigational calculations were suboptimal by modern standards. Yet he built it anyway. Why? Because, like your great-grandmother's butterfly, like your father's collection of license plates, like your family's chaotic movement pattern, he was following an emotional frequency he couldn't articulate. The lighthouse is old enough that it's been calling to you across decades. You've just finally arrived to answer.*

Aaliyah didn't share the message immediately. Instead, she stepped forward, toward the source of the humming, and let her family follow because that's what they did—they followed her calculations and her fear and her desperate certainty that she could make sense of senseless things if she just understood the underlying pattern.

The base of the lighthouse was circular, and the stone was warm beneath her palm when she reached out and touched it. Warm and vibrating and singing, definitely singing, a frequency so low it was almost below her consciousness but so present it was impossible to ignore.

"Everyone come here," she called. "All of you. Together."

Her father came first—Anthony always came first when Aaliyah asked for something, moving with the kind of faith that suggested he'd decided weeks ago to trust his daughter's instincts as completely as she was learning to trust his. Her mother arrived next, journal in one hand, extending her other hand to Aaliyah. Elijah practically dragged himself and Sully to the lighthouse base, and the moment they were all touching the stone—all five points of their family frequency simultaneously in contact with a structure that had been calling for more than a century—the humming changed.

It became music.

Not metaphorically. Literally. The lighthouse was producing sound waves in patterns that resolved into something that resembled melody, resonated at frequencies that matched the Córdoba plaza, the rose window, the gravity-inverted garden. All of them singing the same song, just in different registers, different languages of temporal mathematics.

"It's measuring us," Luisana whispered. She was holding Aaliyah's hand and her husband's hand and writing in her journal simultaneously, which suggested her brain was so activated it had figured out how to multitask at genius level. "It's calibrating. It's checking that you're still on frequency."

"We are," Aaliyah said. And she knew it was true the way she knew her own name was true, the way she knew her father would book flights without a plan and her mother would find the best coffee and her brother would understand what dogs were thinking: because the frequency was real, it was part of her, it was beating in her chest like a second heartbeat that belonged to everyone simultaneously.

The lighthouse beam swept across them, and Aaliyah watched the light separate into component colors—not just visible spectrum but something beyond, frequencies her eyes shouldn't have been able to perceive but somehow could. Red and green and violet and colors that didn't have names because human language hadn't invented vocabulary for what happened when time learned how to sing.

Her phone buzzed again. She felt it rather than heard it, vibrating against her thigh.

*Ninety-six percent. Your resonance frequency has increased. Twelve days until perfect synchronization. This is also a message: these anchors—the lighthouse, the plaza, all of them—they've been waiting for you across time. They recognize your frequency because you were always going to come here. This isn't prediction. This is inevitability. You were always moving toward Curaçao. The world's temporal fragmentation was always going to need exactly your family to mend it. From the moment of your birth, Aaliyah, every decision has pointed you here, to this lighthouse, to this moment.*

Aaliyah felt something shift in her chest. Not frightening, but significant. The difference between moving toward a destination by choice and realizing you'd never had a choice at all, that everything was already written in the frequency of your family's heartbeat.

"Is it okay?" her father asked quietly. He could read her expressions like a book, had always been able to. "Does the lighthouse bring good news?"

Aaliyah looked at her family—all of them connected, all of them singing with the lighthouse, all of them burning with the kind of frequency that could reshape reality if it needed to. She thought about inevitability and destiny and whether the difference mattered if the destination was somewhere you wanted to go anyway.

"Yeah," she said. "Yeah, it's good news. The lighthouse says we're right. That we're exactly where we need to be."

And for once, she actually believed it.

The beam swept across them again, illuminating them all in colors that hadn't existed yesterday, singing a frequency that had been waiting more than a century to welcome them home.
